coredata.dc:title: Non-instrumental and ultrasensitive detection of Acetamiprid residue based on Tyndall effect of silver nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: SANG, Xue-Qing
YAN, Wen-Ju
QIN, Xue-Fei
ZHOU, Xue-Ying
JIN, Wen-Ying
YUAN, Ya-Li
ZHANG, Yun
NIU, Wen-Xin
coredata.dc:description: Pesticide detection is of great significance in the fields of food safety and environmental monitoring. Herein, an unprecedented and facile non-instrumental detection strategy for pesticide residues was developed based on the Tyndall effect. The silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) protected by specifically designed DNA aptamer were applied as the signal producer, and their different aggregation state induced by the addition of pesticide target were sensitively observed and evaluated through the Tyndall effect with the aid of merely a laser pointer. Combining with the pesticide target, the DNA chain folded up to certain conformation which rendered the Ag NPs a more dispersed and stable state, leading to weaker scattering and thus the weaker Tyndall effect. Taking advantage of this phenomenon, a relationship between pesticide concentration and intensity of Tyndall effect was established. Experimental results showed that the concentration of pesticide target, taking acetamiprid as representative, has a good linear relationship with gray value of Tyndall effect in the range of 3.4 × 10−9 ∼ 1.03 × 10−6 mol/L, with the detection limit of 1.2 nmol/L under optimum conditions selected through the orthogonal design. Furthermore, the satisfying results of anti-interference and recovery assay manifested the admirable practicality of this approach for real samples. Getting rid of complicated detection processes and expensive instruments, our strategy might open up a promising prospect for on-site and household detection of contamination with simple operation, low cost, and high sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-instrumental detection
Tyndall effect
Pesticide
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of non-instrumental detection platform for acetamiprid
Study of Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in systems at different state
Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in salt solution
Tyndall effect of Ag NPs protected by DNA
Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in the presence of DNA and acetamiprid
Analytical performance of Tyndall effect-based acetamiprid assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of non-instrumental assay of acetamiprid based on Tyndall effect of Ag NPs.

(a) UV-Vis absorption of Ag NPs; (b) DLS image of Ag NPs; (c) TEM of Ag NPs (scale bars: 25 nm); (d) Tyndall effect photo of Ag NPs solution. Inset: gray value (GV) of light path in photo measured by Image J.

(a) UV-Vis absorption, (b) solution photos and Tyndall effect images of Ag NPs solution in addition of increasing NaCl concentration of 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mol/L. (c) The MGV of Tyndall effect images in (b). (d) UV-Vis absorption, (e) solution photos and Tyndall effect images of Ag NPs solution in addition of increasing DNA concentration of 0, 5, 10, 15 µmol/L at 0.8 mol/L NaCl. (f) The MGV of Tyndall effect images in (e).

(a) UV-Vis absorption, (b) solution photos and Tyndall effect images of Ag NPs solution in presence of increasing acetamiprid concentrations of 0, 5, and 10 µmol/L with 0.8 mol/L NaCl and 15 µmol/L DNA. (c) Zeta potential of three solutions: acetamiprid, Ag NPs with DNA, and acetamiprid together with Ag NPs and DNA. TEM images of Ag NPs (d) with and (e) without the presence of acetamiprid. Scale bars: 50 nm.

(a) Tyndall effect images of 16 tests in orthogonal experiment (left: sample solution, right: blank solution). (b) Real photos and Tyndall effect images of Ag NPs solution with different concentrations of acetamiprid. (c) UV-Vis absorption of Ag NPs solution with different concentrations of acetamiprid. Inset: linear plot of UV-Vis absorption and acetamiprid concentration. (d) MGV of Ag NPs solution with different concentrations of acetamiprid. Inset: linear relationship between MGV and acetamiprid concentration. (e) MGV of solutions in presence of 1.03 µmol/L acetamiprid with the addition of 10 µmol/L imidacloprid, 10 µmol/L thiacloprid, 20 µmol/L prometryn, 20 µmol/L simazine, and 20 µmol/L glucose separately.
Detection results of acetamiprid in tap water and lettuce.
Analytical properties of varied methods for acetamiprid detection.

head.ce:title: Non-instrumental and ultrasensitive detection of Acetamiprid residue based on Tyndall effect of silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Detection, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Detection, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cState Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticide detection is of great significance in the fields of food safety and environmental monitoring. Herein, an unprecedented and facile non-instrumental detection strategy for pesticide residues was developed based on the Tyndall effect. The silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) protected by specifically designed DNA aptamer were applied as the signal producer, and their different aggregation state induced by the addition of pesticide target were sensitively observed and evaluated through the Tyndall effect with the aid of merely a laser pointer. Combining with the pesticide target, the DNA chain folded up to certain conformation which rendered the Ag NPs a more dispersed and stable state, leading to weaker scattering and thus the weaker Tyndall effect. Taking advantage of this phenomenon, a relationship between pesticide concentration and intensity of Tyndall effect was established. Experimental results showed that the concentration of pesticide target, taking acetamiprid as representative, has a good linear relationship with gray value of Tyndall effect in the range of 3.4 × 10−9 ∼ 1.03 × 10−6 mol/L, with the detection limit of 1.2 nmol/L under optimum conditions selected through the orthogonal design. Furthermore, the satisfying results of anti-interference and recovery assay manifested the admirable practicality of this approach for real samples. Getting rid of complicated detection processes and expensive instruments, our strategy might open up a promising prospect for on-site and household detection of contamination with simple operation, low cost, and high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are indispensable in modern agriculture, however, the excessive use or abuse of pesticides raises wide public health concerns due to the toxicity accumulation through food chain in ecosystem [1]. For example, acetamiprid, used by spraying on vegetables and fruits, can lead to the normal conduction failure of the central nervous system after insect exposure or ingestion acetamiprid due to its selective binding to acetylcholine receptors and competes with insect acetylcholine [2,3]. If people ingest or are exposed to acetamiprid, it will damage primary neurons by inducing necrosis and oxidative stress, leading to symptoms of autonomic neural excitation, and causing damage in various tissues of the body [4–6]. Traditional analytical techniques include gas chromatography (GC) [7,8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], and mass spectrometry (MS) [11], and GC/LC-MS is commonly considered as the gold standard [12], even though it is not user-friendly in the perspective of requiring expensive equipment and professional operation. Subsequently, other detection strategies with added merits have been developed, such as surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [13], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [14–16], electrochemical [17–19] and fluorescence [20,21] detection with the help of different nanomaterials. These methods have made certain progress in terms of detection time and operation simplicity, but are still not viable for on-site detection due to the dependence on professional instruments. Colorimetric analysis, especially those merely involving naked-eye without ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy [22,23], have emerged to solve the limitation and are in real sense able to achieve non-instrumental detection. However, this method has suffered from the drawbacks of low sensitivity and accuracy owing to different color discrimination ability of human eyes. Therefore, it is highly pivotal to develop new methodology for sensitive, portable, cost effective, and on-site determination of pesticide residues.

Tyndall effect is the light scattering of particles in a colloid or suspension, based on which a bright light path may form in liquid or gas system. It is highly interlinked with the wavelength of incident light and the size of particles according to the Mie Theory and Rayleigh scattering equation [24]. If the presence of certain molecules can induce the particle size change, like well dispersion to aggregation [25] or decomposition [26], varied intensity of Tyndall effect can be observed under the same incident light. So it is reasonable to propose the detection protocol based on Tyndall effect by proper evaluation of its intensity. As far as we know, very few of the Tyndall effect relating analysis have been hitherto reported. Our group have made the first step, by quantifying gray value of the Tyndall effect photo, equipped with a laser pointer and a smartphone and carried out in a handmade cardboard box [27]. Zhou's group have developed a dual-mode method for the detection of fluorinated pesticide through ratiometric fluorescence and Tyndall effect methods [28]. Furthermore, the successful detection of metal ions [29] and small molecules [30] make the proposal of other analyte assay, including pesticide, through Tyndall effect solid. This sensing strategy has admirable advantages in terms of portability and simplicity, showing great potential in the field of facile and instrument-free detection.

Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) present unique optical, thermal, magnetic, and electrical properties due to its surface effects and macroscopic quantum tunneling effect. The properties exhibited by Ag NPs are closely related to their morphology, size, crystallinity and structure [31,32], which can be easily tuned by different synthesis conditions and methods. Ag NPs are now widely used in the fields of biosensing [33], electrocatalysis [34,35], photoelectronics [36] and bioimaging [37,38], and are also acting as potential candidate of optical functional materials. Taking advantage of the excellent light scattering properties of Ag NPs, Ling et al. [39] created a visual analytical method to explore the interaction between ligands and analyte. Chavada et al. [40] synthesized the pyrophosphate functionalized Ag NPs for colorimetric determination of deferiprone through competitive binding to Fe3+ between deferiprone and Ag NPs. Yousefi et al. [41] established a novel fluorescent aptasensor, in which the fluorescence of doped carbon quantum dots was affected by the different aggregation of Ag NPs. However, the Tyndall effect of Ag NPs has rarely been studied in sensing field yet.

Herein, we construct a novel system involving Tyndall effect of Ag NPs that can realize sensitive, portable and non-instrumental monitoring of pesticide, with the representative of acetamiprid. The Ag NPs simply reduced by citrate with no further functionalization are applied as the particles producing Tyndall effect. As shown in Scheme 1 , the single DNA, which is also designed as the aptamer of target, is used to keep Ag NPs isolated and steady. In the solution of high salt concentration, the DNA protection toward Ag NPs is greatly impaired and the aggregation of Ag NPs happens, giving rise to intense Tyndall effect. With the addition of acetamiprid, the DNA shows preferential binding to acetamiprid and forms a complex in the specific conformation. Intriguingly, the obtained complex in such state exhibit stronger protection toward Ag NPs, different from the other cases that the combination of DNA aptamer with target leads to no protection surface and thus aggregation of Au NPs [25]. So in the presence of acetamiprid, the solution system displays weaker Tyndall effect. After reaction, Tyndall effect of different solutions was recorded by a smart phone and the gray value of the light path in photos are evaluated with Image J as the quantitative readout. While environmental interference factors tend to induce aggregation of Ag NPs, only the addition of target improves the monodispersity and stability of Ag NPs, which greatly reduces the likelihood of false positive signal. Another merit of this proposed strategy is that the range of detection pesticide is considerably expanded by easily tuning the sequence of DNA to different aptamers.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Trisodium citrate, ascorbic acid (AA) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were bought from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. Acetamiprid, imidacloprid (98.5%), prometryn, simazine and thiacloprid were obtained from Aladdin Biochem Technology Co. Ltd. Aptamer of acetamiprid (5′-TGT AAT TTG TCT GCA GCG GTT CTT GAT CGC TGA CAC CAT ATT ATG AAG A-3′) was purchased from Sangon Biotech-nology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a TU-1950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Pu Analytical General instrument Co., Ltd., China). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) morphology of Ag NPs was observed on an FEI Tecnai G2 f20s-twin field emission TEM instrument (FEI Co., USA). The Tyndall effect signals were produced by a 635 nm red laser pointer pen (Deli Group Co., Ltd., China). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were conducted using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). All images of colorimetric results were conducted using a smartphone.

Monodispersed Ag NPs were simply synthesized by adding the mixture of AgNO3 and citrate into AA solution [42]. 200 µL of 4 mM AA was first added into 95 mL boiling water and kept boiling for 1 min. 0.5 mL of 15 mM AgNO3 and 5 mL of 6.8 mM citrate solution were subsequently mixed with the obtained AA solution at boiling state. The colorless mixture gradually turned to light yellow, and was further boiled under stirring for 1 h, ensuring the formation of uniform quasi-sphere Ag NPs. The obtained Ag NPs solution was used directly for Tyndall effect application without other processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of non-instrumental detection platform for acetamiprid
ce:section.ce:para: 100 µL of a series of acetamiprid solution with different concentrations were separately mixed with 100 µL of 15 µM DNA in 150 µL buffer (10 mM Tris with 20 mM KCl, pH 7.4) at room temperature for 30 min, followed by the addition of 1 mL Ag NPs solution. After 20 min incubation, 100 µL of 0.8 M NaCl was added into the obtained mixture and reacted for 15 min. The Tyndall effect of the final solution was observed in a homemade black box with a fixed laser pointer inside. A smartphone was used to take the photo, the gray value of which was analyzed by Image J and was further used as a measure of Tyndall effect intensity.

The practical application of this method was evaluated by taking tap water and lettuce as real samples. The tap water was obtained directly from our lab and treated only with filtration before determination. Lettuce was purchased from the supermarket, and then was cut and smashed into puree state, followed by the addition of 10 mL H2O. The diluted mixture was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was treated with 0.22 µm filtering membrane and ready to be used in following detection. Spiked samples were prepared by adding standard acetamiprid solution into treated tap water and lettuce sample solution. The detection process was the same as the procedure in Section 2.3.

To achieve the non-instrumental detection of acetamiprid based on Tyndall effect, Ag NPs are first synthesized by the simple citrate reduction method and characterized to check their morphology. The yellow solution has an obvious and sharp UV-Vis absorption peak at 412 nm (Fig. 1 a), indicating the good monodispersity of obtained Ag NPs. DLS and TEM data showed the consistent result that the size of Ag NPs is around 25.4 nm in the sphere shape (Fig. 1b,c), possible as the candidate probe of Tyndall effect. As further manifested in Fig. 1d, the uniformly dispersed Ag NPs in solution form a clear light path exposed to laser at the wavelength of 635 nm, implying the Rayleigh scattering is the main physical mechanism behind the Tyndall effect in our system [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in systems at different state
Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in salt solution
ce:section.ce:para: The detection strategy in this article is taking the Tyndall effect intensity of Ag NPs in different aggregation state as the quantitative readout to measure the targets which cause disturbance to the dispersion of Ag NPs. Whether the system involving factors like salt, DNA and acetamiprid can work as expected needs to be studied and we will start from the simplest case, Ag NPs in salt solution.

By citrate reduction method, the obtained Ag NPs surface is wrapped only with negatively charged citrate ligand and high salt concentration can cause certain aggregation of Ag NPs. Fig. 2 a shows that with increasing salt concentration, the characteristic absorption peak of Ag NPs at 412 nm gradually becomes weak and wide, and the solution color also changes from yellow to colorless (Fig. 2b), implying the increasing aggregation of Ag NPs. According to Rayleigh scattering law [44], the stronger aggregation of Ag NPs (bigger particle size) can produce the stronger Tyndall effect, i.e. the higher mean gray value (MGV) of the light path taken in photo. Both of the phenomena are seen in Fig. 2b,c as expected. It is also noted that the aggregation of Ag NPs reaches a plateau at a certain NaCl concentration (0.80 mol/L) and then changes slightly at higher NaCl concentration. This inflexion point concentration can be used for later study in view of the stable state at certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tyndall effect of Ag NPs protected by DNA
ce:section.ce:para: When DNA is added into solution, it will be pulled to Ag NPs surface due to the van der Waals force between them [45]. Thus the negatively charged phosphate group of DNA is expected to increase the electrostatic repulsion between Ag NPs, leading to better anti-aggregation performance of Ag NPs in certain salt solution. When added NaCl is 0.8 mol/L, the concentration at which Ag NPs has the strongest Tyndall effect (Fig. 2b), the UV-Vis absorption of Ag NPs increases little with the DNA concentration changing from 0 to 15 µmol/L (Fig. 2d), and the color and Tyndall effect of the four solution show the similar result (Fig. 2e,f). It implies that the protection of DNA toward Ag NPs truly exists, but is quite weak at this salt concentration and not qualified to construct a quantitative sensor based on the variation of DNA concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tyndall effect of Ag NPs in the presence of DNA and acetamiprid
ce:section.ce:para: The involved DNA is designed as the aptamer of acetamiprid, so it will fold into a specific conformation in the presence of acetamiprid. The resulted complex has a stronger protection toward Ag NPs than single DNA in salt solution, which can be seen clearly in Fig. 3 . The absorption peak of Ag NPs increases obviously as the added acetamiprid concentration rising higher (Fig. 3a), together with the solution color changing from colorless to yellow and the Tyndall effect becoming weaker (Fig. 3b). This result is preliminary explained by the Zeta potential test. The apparent Zeta potential of Ag NPs with DNA was −25.8 mV, and it increases to −32.8 mV when the target is introduced to the system (Fig. 3c). Considering acetamiprid molecule has quite low Zeta potential of 0.72 mV, it is speculated that the combination of DNA with target in this case does not leave Ag NPs alone, on the contrary, DNA assigns more negative charge to Ag NPs, probably because the folded DNA renders closer distance between phosphate groups and Ag NPs surface. Consequently, the complex of DNA and target enhances the electrostatic repulsion between Ag NPs, and hence better dispersity. TEM images of Ag NPs systems before and after incubation with acetamiprid (Fig. 3d, e) also demonstrate the same truth that the addition of target facilitates the free and stable dispersion of Ag NPs.

To achieve the best performance of the sensing platform, all conditions affecting the Tyndall effect have to be comprehensively optimized. According to the aforementioned discussion, concentrations of DNA, NaCl and Ag NPs are the three key parameters and are screened through an orthogonal design with 3 factors and 4 levels, as shown in Tables 1 and 2 . For convenience, the concentrations shown in table are all of the separate solution, not in the final mixing solution. The optimization is carried out by mixing 100 µL DNA, 1 mL Ag NPs and 100 µL NaCl solution in a series of concentration respectively with the fixed 100 µL acetamiprid of 10 µmol/L. The images of Tyndall effect of all these solutions are recorded in Fig. 4 a. The MGV difference between the target mixture and blank solution (∆MGV) is taken as the evaluation standard to calculate and find out the optimum condition. Based on the calculation of sum, average and range of all ∆MGV values for solutions of each factor at different levels, it is found that the three factors, NaCl, DNA, Ag NPs, have varying influence on Tyndall effect of solutions. Thereinto, NaCl has the lowest effect while DNA has the highest impact on the dispersion state change of Ag NPs in this system. For NaCl, only when its concentration gets to a certain high level, the presence or absence of target can produce discernible change in dispersion state of Ag NPs. The concentration of NaCl is at M level, while other two factors are in µmol/L or even lower level. So the system is not so sensitive to the change of NaCl concentration, and hence NaCl has the lowest influence to Ag NPs state. On the contrary, DNA attaches directly on Ag NPs surface and greatly increases the negative charge of Ag NPs. As a result, DNA has a stronger influence on Tyndall effect signal than NaCl. The optimized concentrations are finally selected as 10 µmol/L for DNA, 0.2 mol/L for NaCl, and 0.8 times of original concentration for Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Tyndall effect-based acetamiprid assay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental condition, non-instrumental analysis of acetamiprid are investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 4b–d, the Tyndall effect of sample solution attenuate obviously as the acetamiprid concentration increasing, along with the stronger absorption peak at around 412 nm (Fig. 4c). The linear relationship between acetamiprid concentration and the MGV of corresponding solution is established. The linear range is 3.4 × 10−9 – 1.03 × 10−6 mol/L (R 2 = 0.991), with the detection limit of 1.2 nmol/L, which is much lower than the permissible residue limit of acetamiprid in food (89 nmol/L) stipulated by China [46]. For comparison, the linear range based on UV-Vis absorption of Ag NPs is also calculated (inset of Fig. 4c). Apparently, the narrow range obtained by UV-Vis method, 1.4 × 10−7 – 1.03 × 10−6 mol/L with low correlation coefficient (R 2=0.927), implies the Tyndall effect-based method is more competitive than the UV-Vis method.

To evaluate the selectivity of this novel colorimetric sensor toward acetamiprid, interference study are performed under the same conditions in the presence of potential interfering molecules, including pesticides with similar structure to acetamiprid. 1.03 µmol/L acetamiprid is mixed with 10 µmol/L imidacloprid, 10 µmol/L thiacloprid, 20 µmol/L prometryn, 20 µmol/L simazine, and 20 µmol/L glucose separately, and the MGV of these solutions are measured. As displayed in Fig. 4e, all the interference showed almost no influence on the MGV, manifesting that this strategy shows satisfying selectivity toward acetamiprid.

Furthermore, the analytical performance of the proposed sensor in real sample is investigated. Tap water and lettuce are selected as practical samples and spiked with certain concentration of acetamiprid. As shown in Table 3 , it is found that there is no acetamiprid residual in vegetables and tap water, which is confirmed by the HPLC result. The recovery of the spiked samples is between 90.25%−110%, demonstrating the feasibility of this non-instrumental method in practical samples. For comparison, the detection performance, i.e. linear range and detection limit, achieved by other methods in recent reports are summarized in Table 4 . It is found that our proposed strategy has comparable linear range and even lower detection limit among existing methods, arguing the good practicability of this technique.

Tyndall effect, an interesting optical phenomenon studied for over a century, is now applied for non-instrumental analysis of acetamiprid for the first time. The target induced dispersion state change of Ag NPs is translated into discernible change in Tyndall effect. A laser pointer, a homemade black box, and a smartphone compose the portable detection platform, and achieve results with admirable selectivity and sensitivity. Such performance makes this strategy a promising candidate for point-of-care testing in food and environmental monitoring. Besides, the introduction of DNA into the detection system broadens the analysis target range, bringing great potential of wide application in other fields. Our proposal may open new thinking of fast test, and provide different perspective of exploring the colorimetric analysis realm.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2020GXNSFBA297116).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mostafalou S., Abdollahi M. Pesticides: an update of human exposure and toxicity. Arch Toxicol 2017; 91: 549–599.
Chen M., Tao L., McLean J., Lu C. Quantitative Analysis of Neonicotinoid Insecticide Residues in Foods: Implication for Dietary Exposures. J Agric Food Chem 2014; 62: 6082–6090.

Wang X.L., Qiu J., Xu Y.Y., Liao G.Q., Jia Q., Pan Y.C., et al. Integrated non-targeted lipidomics and metabolomics analyses for fluctuations of neonicotinoids imidacloprid and acetamiprid on Neuro-2a cells. Environ Pollut 2021; 284: 117327.
Hu X.M., Guo J.Y., Tian Y.Research progress on acetamiprid toxicity and exposure. J Environ Occup Med 2019; 36: 595–601.

Gasmi S., Kebieche M., Rouabhi R., Touahria C., Lahouel A., Lakroun Z., et al. Alteration of membrane integrity and respiratory function of brain mitochondria in the rats chronically exposed to a low dose of acetamiprid. Environ Sci Pollut Res 2017; 24: 22258–22264.
Christen V., Rusconi M., Crettaz P., Fent K. Developmental neurotoxicity of different pesticides in PC-12 cells in vitro. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2017; 325: 25–36.

Gawel M., Kiljanek T., Niewiadowska A., Semeniuk S., Goliszek M., Burek O., et al. Determination of neonicotinoids and 199 other pesticide residues in honey by liquid and gas chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry. Food Chem. 2019; 282: 36–47.

Cacho J.I., Campillo N., Vinas P., Hernandez-Cordoba M. In situ ionic liquid dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction coupled to gas chromatography-mass spectrometry for the determination of organophosphorus pesticides. J Chromatogr A 2018; 1559: 95–101.

Ma J.P., Wu G.G., Li S., Tan W.Q., Wang X.Y., Li J.H., et al. Magnetic solid-phase extraction of heterocyclic pesticides in environmental water samples using metal-organic frameworks coupled to high performance liquid chromatography determination. J Chromatogr A 2018; 1553: 57–66.

Hanna N., Sun P., Sun Q., Li X.W., Yang X.W., Ji X., et al. Presence of antibiotic residues in various environmental compartments of Shandong province in eastern China: Its potential for resistance development and ecological and human risk. Environ Int 2018; 114: 131–142.

Huang Y.S., Shi T., Luo X., Xiong H.L., Min F.F., Chen Y., et al. Determination of multi-pesticide residues in green tea with a modified QuEChERS protocol coupled to HPLC-MS/MS. Food Chem. 2019; 275: 255–264.

Jovanov P., Guzsvany V., Franko M., Lazic S., Sakac M., Saric B., et al. Multi-residue method for determination of selected neonicotinoid insecticides in honey using optimized dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction combined with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Talanta 2013; 111: 125–133.
Wang K.Q., Sun D.W., Pu H.B., Wei Q.Y. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering of core-shell Au@Ag nanoparticles aggregates for rapid detection of difenoconazole in grapes. Talanta 2019; 191: 449–456.
Chen Z.J., Huang Z.C., Sun Y.M., Xu Z.L., Liu J.W. The Most Active Oxidase-Mimicking Mn2O3 Nanozyme for Biosensor Signal Generation. Chem Eur J 2021; 27: 9597–9604.
Zhu Y.Y., Wu J.J.X., Han L.J., Wang X.Y., Li W., Guo H.C., et al. Nanozyme Sensor Arrays Based on Heteroatom-Doped Graphene for Detecting Pesticides. Anal Chem 2020; 92: 7444–7452.
Zhu D.Q., Zhang M.L., Pu L., Gai P.P., Li F. Nitrogen-Enriched Conjugated Polymer Enabled Metal-Free Carbon Nanozymes with Efficient Oxidase-Like Activity. Small 2022; 18: 2104993.

Karimi-Maleh H., Yola M.L., Atar N., Orooji Y., Karimi F., Kumar P.S., et al. A novel detection method for organophosphorus insecticide fenamiphos: Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on core-shell Co3O4@MOF-74 nanocomposite. J Colloid Interface Sci 2021; 592: 174–185.
